coredata.dc:title: Boron-doped carbon nanoparticles: Size-independent color tunability from red to blue and bioimaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sadhanala, Hari Krishna
Nanda, Karuna Kar
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of boron-doped CNPs (B-CNPs) with different size/atomic percentage of doping and size-independent color tunability from red to blue. The variation of size/atomic percentage of B is achieved by simply varying the reaction time, while the color tunability is obtained by diluting the solution. With dilution, the luminescence spectra are not only blue-shifted, the intensity increases as well. The huge blue-shift in the emission energy (∼1 eV) is believed to be due to the increase in the interparticle distance. The quantum yield with optimum dilution is found to increase with boron doping though it is very low as compared to CNPs and nitrogen-doped CNPs. Finally, we show that B-CNPs with a quantum yield of 0.5% can be used for bioimaging applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of boron doped carbon nanoparticles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Department of Science and Technology (DST) ( 1046 ) for the financial support and IISc for 200 kV FETEM facility. The authors also acknowledge the help of Siva Krishna Narra for bioimaging experiments in their Laboratory. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM images of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. (d) Diameter as a function of reaction time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–e) Raman, XRD and high resolution B 1s XPS spectra of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. (f) B/C atomic ratio as a function of reaction time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) UV–visible and (b–d) PL spectra of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 with different excitations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) Dilution effect on PL of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. (d–f) PL intensity variation vs. dilution of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. (g–i) Normalized PL of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 without and with maximum dilution with 365 nm excitation. Insets of 4f-i show optical images under UV lamp (365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

HeLa cells incubated (a) without and (b–d) with BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. Left and right rows are bright field images and fluorescent images in the dark field, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Yeast cells incubated (a) without and (b–d) with BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. Left and right rows are bright field images and fluorescent images in the dark field, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Samples prepared from 0.8 g (2 wt %) of boric acid and 0.8 g (2 wt %) of sucrose.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped carbon nanoparticles: Size-independent color tunability from red to blue and bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Research Centre, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of boron-doped CNPs (B-CNPs) with different size/atomic percentage of doping and size-independent color tunability from red to blue. The variation of size/atomic percentage of B is achieved by simply varying the reaction time, while the color tunability is obtained by diluting the solution. With dilution, the luminescence spectra are not only blue-shifted, the intensity increases as well. The huge blue-shift in the emission energy (∼1 eV) is believed to be due to the increase in the interparticle distance. The quantum yield with optimum dilution is found to increase with boron doping though it is very low as compared to CNPs and nitrogen-doped CNPs. Finally, we show that B-CNPs with a quantum yield of 0.5% can be used for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the band gap which largely controls the optical and electrical properties of a semiconductor can be modified by varying the pressure [1,2], temperature [3,4], doping concentration [5], composition [6–8] and size of the nanocrystals [9]. The pressure, temperature and doping can change the band gap by few meV, while the change can be very large when the composition and the size are varied. For example, the luminescence of CdSe quantum dot is tuned from red to blue by varying the diameter from 12 to 2 nm [10]. Similarly, the band gap of CdSSe can be tuned from 2.36 to 1.79 eV by simply varying precursor ratio of S and Se [7]. One of the interesting applications is that nanocrystals have the potential to emit in the complete visible range so that an ultraviolet light emitting diode can be coated to obtain white light obviating the need to blend two or more dots of differing size [11]. The other applications such as photodetectors [12], photovoltaic cells [13,14] etc. also depend on the optical and electrical properties and hence, on the band gap.

Recently, carbon based nanostructured materials have gained popularity because of cheap, low-toxicity, biocompatible and can easily be synthesized in a variety of morphology [15–17]. Among the carbon based nanostructured materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [9], carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) [18,19], N-doped CNPs (N-CNPs) [20] emit in the blue-green region. It has been shown that the luminescence is size-dependent and the color can be tuned from red to blue by varying the diameter from 3.8 to 1.2 nm [21]. As a consequence, these nanostructures have drawn much attention because of their exciting applications such as bioimaging [22–25], biosensing [26] and light emitting diodes [27–29]. In this regard, B-doped CNPs (B-CNPs) has not explored yet as only few reports are there on boron doped graphene [30], graphene quantum dots [31,32] and CNTs [33] because of difficulties in the synthesis. Solvothermal technique [34,35] and electrochemical method [31] are recently reported for the synthesis of B-CNPs. Here, we report for the first time the hydrothermal synthesis of B-CNPs from sucrose as carbon and boric acid as boron source materials at 180 °C. We have shown that the emission from red to blue can be tuned by interparticle distance, which were achieved simply by diluting the solution. With dilution, the luminescence spectra are not only blue-shifted but also the intensity increases followed by a decrease in the intensity. We show that the size and boron doping can be controlled by simply varying the reaction time and the quantum yield is found to increase with boron doping. We also show that B-CNPs with a quantum yield (QY) of 0.5% can be used for bioimaging applications.
Both boric acid and sucrose were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and NaOH pallets were obtained from Spectrochem chemicals. Millipore water was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron doped carbon nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of B-CNPs, 0.8 g (2 wt %) of boric acid and 0.8 g (2 wt %) of sucrose were added to 40 ml of deionised water. Then, the reaction solution was transferred to 50 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and it was kept in muffle furnace for 3, 6 and 10 h at 180 °C. After that, the autoclave was left for cool down to room temperature naturally. We would like to note that unreacted B2O3 is found in the obtained brown color B-CNPs solution (Fig. S1) which is removed by washing with 1 M NaOH solution [30]. Finally, B-CNPs solution was collected by applying centrifugation. We denoted B-CNPs prepared at 3, 6 and 10 h as BC-3, BC-6 and BC-10 before and BC-31, BC-61 and BC101 after washing with NaOH for simplicity.

The product is characterized by JEOL transmission electron microscopy (TEM), A. P. E. Research A-100 atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ‘X'pert Pro PAnalytical X-ray diffraction (XRD), PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), WITec Raman spectroscopy system with 532 nm excitation laser and Horiba Jobin Yvon fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer with Xe lamp as excitation source, Hitachi U-2900 UV–visible spectrophotometer and Zeiss Apotome fluorescence microscope with 100× objective Lens.

For QY measurement, we prepare quinine sulfate (QS) solution containing 0.05 M H2SO4 as a standard and B-CNPs with optimum luminescence intensity are obtained by sufficient dilution to avoid the re-absorption effect. Then, absorption and PL of QS and B-CNPs are measured by using UV–Visible Hitachi 2900 absorption spectrometer and Horiba Jobin Yvon fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. After that, QY of B-CNPs is calculated by following equation
Φ
B − CNPs
=
Φ
QS
(
I
B − CNPs
/
I
QS
)
(
η
QS
/
η
B − CNPs
)
(
A
QS
/
A
B − CNPs
)
,
where ΦB-CNPs and ΦQS are QY of B-CNPs and QS, I is the integrated area under PL curve, η is the refractive index of solvent and A is the absorption value.

Fig. 2 a shows Raman spectra of B-CNPs. All the spectra exhibit well defined D peak (∼1362 cm−1), G peak (∼1595 cm−1), and 2D peak (∼2543 cm−1) which are common peaks for every nanocarbon system. ID/IG ratio of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 are found to be 0.95, 0.99 and 1.05 which suggests the incorporation of boron atoms in graphene lattice system enhances the ratio. Fig. 2b shows the typical XRD pattern of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 with a broad peak around 24.46° that corresponds to a d-spacing of 0.363 nm, which is higher than the distance between (002) planes of graphite (0.34 nm) and is believed to be due to oxygen related functional groups [37]. FT-IR spectra of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 as presented in Fig. S3 reveal the presence of B–C and C–O−Na+ stretching (energy bands at 1100–1150 and 1390 cm−1). We also observed two main bands at 1400–1680 and 3200–3600 cm−1 corresponding to –CC- and –O-H functional groups, respectively that are the primary bands for CNPs.

FT-IR study further supported by XPS analysis of B-CNPs. Fig. S4 shows the C 1s, O 1s XPS spectra of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. Fig. 2c–e show high resolution B 1s XPS spectra of BC-3, BC-61 and BC-101 which reveals the presence of B–C sp2, BC2O and BCO2 groups [30]. The three peaks, in Fig. 2c–e, are suggesting that boron atoms present in carbon conjugation and as well as connected to carbon and oxygen atoms. The B–C peak intensity is prominent with reaction time due to increase of conjugation (Fig. S5). The atompic percentage of B, C and O varies with the reaction time (Table 1). Overall, the diameter increases with reaction time [38] as shown in Fig. 2f along with the B/C and O/C ratio (Table 1).

As-synthesized BC-10, which contains B2O3, shows emission peak around 560 nm upon excitation with 365 nm. As shown in Fig. S6, the PL spectrum is red-shifted to 680 nm after washing BC-10 with NaOH solution. Such large bathochromic shift is probably due to the hydroxylate and carboxylate anions generated after washing with NaOH solution resulting in the decrease of band gap [39]. This observation is corroborated by the FT-IR spectra in which the characteristic peak around 1390 cm−1 clearly suggests the presence of carboxylate [39] and hydroxylate groups (Fig. S7).

Further, we explore the optical properties of B-CNPs. The absorption spectra of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 show the similar absorption spectral profiles. The absorption spectra show an intense band at 283 nm and a weak band at 225 nm (Fig. 3 a). The band at 283 nm originates from n - π* electronic transition of CO band, whereas the 225 nm band arises from the π - π* electronic transition of CC band. Interestingly, BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 show excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL), which is similar to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [40] as shown in Fig. 3b–d. As the wavelength is varied from 300 to 550 nm, there is no change in the peak position due to uniform and self-passivated surface states [41]. This is in contrast with that reported for CNPs and N-CNPs [42]. Luminescence of CNPs is reported to be size-dependent [27] as well as excitation-dependent [28]. The band gap of CNPs is reported to increase as the size is decreased below ∼4 nm or so [43]. As the size of B-CNPs is 3.5–6.0 nm, the band gap is expected to be size-independent. However, the results may vary for B-CNPs. It has also been shown that the band gap increases with boron atomic percentage [44] in the case of boron-doped graphene, and a similar trend is expected for B-CNPs. Therefore, a red-shift is expected with reaction time as the size is increases, while a blue-shift can be due to the increase in the boron doping as the reaction time is increased. As shown in Fig S8, the PL peak of B-CNPs are red-shifted as the reaction time is increased which suggests that the size effect [45] is dominant over the influence of boron concentration. In this context, it may be noted that luminescence of B-CNPs can also be controlled by surface oxidation [46].

Fig. 4 a–c show PL spectra of diluted BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101. As the dilution increases, the PL intensity increases followed by a slight decrease (Fig. 4d–f). In addition, a huge blue-shift in the PL spectra is observed as shown in Fig. 4g–i and optical images of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 under UV lamp (365 nm) are shown in insets. Fig. S9a-c shows normalized PL of Fig. 4a–c. The shifting of PL peak position from red to blue region is clearly observed as shown in Fig. S9c. Similar blue-shift and enhancement of intensity have also been observed with different organic solvents such as dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) and tetrahydrofuran (THF). The enhancement in the PL intensity along with the blue-shift is stronger as compared to that with water (Fig. S10a). This is believed to be due to less polar nature of DMF, DMSO and THF and less stabilization of excited state in these organic solvents (Fig. S10b) [47].

Generally, the PL intensity increases with the increase of the concentration up to a certain level and then decreases above that due to self-quenching along with red-shift. It has been reported for CNPs and g-C3N4 that shift of the PL spectra can also be due to protonation/deprotonation [19,48]. As the blue-shift is observed for all kind of solvents, it is our belief that the interparticle distance controls the luminescence properties. At higher concentration, decrease of the interparticle distance enhances the interactions between the particles resulting in the red-shift and decrease of PL intensity was due to the self-quenching. As the solution is diluted, the interparticle distance increases (Fig. S11) and the interaction among the particles decreases, resulting in the blue-shift with increase of PL intensity due to the radiative process. Irrespective of the mechanism, our observation is that luminescence spectra can be tuned by diluting the solution.

The increased brightness is quantified by QY and the QY of BC-31, BC-61 and BC-101 is found to be 0.5, 2.2 and 2.7% (Table 1) by using Quinine Sulfate as reference. It may be noted here that the QY of BC-101 is slightly lower than that of B-CNPs (3.6) prepared electrochemically [31]. Highly luminescent CNPs have been used to mark cells such as breast cancer cells [49], EAC cells [50], HeLa cells [51], RBC cells [52], Escherichia coli cells [53] and yeast cells [54] due to their good biocompatibility and low toxicity. However, the QY is for low for our B-CNPs and we, therefore, carried out the cell imaging in order to find out the minimum QY required for the purpose.

We report the synthesis of B-CNPs with different size/atomic percentage of doping from sucrose and boric acid by using a simple hydrothermal method. The variation of size/atomic percentage of B is achieved by simply varying the reaction time. Interestingly, B-CNPs exhibit excitation independent photoluminescence but the color from red to blue can be tuned by interparticle distance of B-CNPs, which can be achieved simply by dilution. With dilution, the luminescence spectra are not only blue-shifted, but also the intensity increases followed by a slight decrease. The minimization of reabsorption among B-CNPs is responsible for the huge variation in the emission energy. The QY of B-CNPs is varied from 0.5 to 2.7% with the increasing reaction time, which suggests that QY increases with doping concentration. We have shown that HeLa and yeast cells labeling can be done using B-CNPs and B-CNPs with a minimum QY of 0.5% can be used for this purpose.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Department of Science and Technology (DST) (1046) for the financial support and IISc for 200 kV FETEM facility. The authors also acknowledge the help of Siva Krishna Narra for bioimaging experiments in their Laboratory.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary Information available [XRD, AFM, FT-IR, XPS and PL data for different B-CNPs].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.08.096.
